title: Partial & Required
description: Built-in utility types for quick changes.

slides.title: Utility Types 🧰
slides.content:
TypeScript comes packed with beautifully built-in global Utility Types.

These are essentially completely pre-written Generics that take an existing Interface and mathematically transform it into a brand new shape instantly!

slides.title: The `Partial<Type>` Utility
slides.content: `Partial<T>` takes all the properties of an interface and instantly makes every single one of them optional (`?`). It perfectly handles massive profile updates.

quizData.question: If you dynamically pass `{ email: "bob@test.com" }` into the `updates` argument above, will it successfully miraculously compile cleanly?

options.text: Yes, because Partial<User> means you can provide any subset of the User properties.
No, because the original interface strictly structurally requires all three.
Yes, but only if you provide exactly two properties at minimum.

quizData.explanation: Partial converts `id: number` to `id?: number` and so on, enabling you to safely pass one, some, or all properties.

slides.title: The `Required<Type>` Utility
slides.content: `Required<T>` does the exact opposite. It takes an interface that might have optional properties, and mathematically strips away all the `?` marks, strictly forcing everything to be mandatory.

slides.title: The `Readonly<Type>` Utility 🔒
slides.content: There is also `Readonly<T>`, which gracefully transforms every property into a `readonly` property, preventing mutations!
